metadata.content: One of the biggest things I wish I knew earlier is how fast college goes by. I spent so much time worrying about doing everything right that I did not always let myself try things that I was interested in. I overthought opportunities, hesitated to apply for things, and sometimes avoided stuff because I was scared of looking inexperienced. Looking back, I wish I had taken more chances and trusted that I did not need to have everything figured out at first. I also wish I had asked more questions and spent less time comparing myself to other people. By the time you get close to graduation you really realized no one had it all perfectly planned out anyways. And I think my biggest regret is wasting energy trying to seem prepared instead of just learning as I went.
metadata.summary: A first-generation college graduate reflects on their biggest regrets, wishing they had taken more chances and worried less about seeming prepared. They advise students to embrace learning as they go, ask more questions, and avoid comparing themselves to others, recognizing that no one truly has it all figured out.

metadata.content: One thing I really wish I knew before college was how expensive the small things would be. I knew tuition was a big deal, but I didn't think enough about textbooks, food, late night take out, school supplies, club fees and random costs that keep showing up. As a first gen student, I also felt guilty about spending money because I knew how much my family was already sacrificing for me to even be here. I was embarrassed to talk about it, so I just tried to handle it all quietly. What I wish I knew is that budgeting really makes a huge difference. Even if it is just tracking where your money goes every week. I also wish I had known sooner that there are campus resources, cheaper textbook options and people who can actually help.
metadata.summary: This first-generation college graduate recounts the financial stress caused by unexpected "small" college expenses beyond tuition, like textbooks and club fees. Driven by guilt over family sacrifices, they initially struggled in silence but ultimately learned the value of budgeting and leveraging campus resources for support.

metadata.content: As a first generation student, and the only one of my family to make it to college, I wish I knew how heavy the pressure would feel. My parents came to this country at my age without knowing the language, and I carry that with me everyday. It makes me feel like I can't fail because they sacrificed so much for me to have opportunities they didn't. The pressure can be very hard sometimes, but I've learned to use it to push myself and thrive instead of letting it break me. I remind myself that their journey and mine are different, and I don't have to be perfect. I would tell other first gen students that it's okay to feel the pressure, but don't let it consume you and use it as a way to motivate you.
metadata.summary: As a first-generation college student, the author describes the immense pressure felt due to their immigrant parents' sacrifices and the desire to succeed. They've learned to channel this weight into motivation, reminding themselves that their journey is unique and perfection isn't required. The author encourages other first-gen students to use pressure as a drive, not a burden.

metadata.content: I remember sitting in my dorm freshman year scrolling through LinkedIn and it felt like everyone already knew what they were doing. Uh people were talking about internships, networking, referrals and I didn't even know what those words really meant. My parents didn't get into college so I didn't have anyone to ask. I thought I was just behind like I had already somehow failed. Sophomore year I almost didn't apply to any internships because I assumed I wasn't qualified, but one night I randomly went to a career workshop. Mostly for the free food, but that's where I learned that no one actually expects you to know everything. You're just kind of supposed to figure it out as you go. I applied to 27 internships and got rejected from 26, but that one yes changed everything. What I wish I knew earlier is this, um everyone looks like they have a plan, but most people are just guessing confidently. Uh don't count yourself out before you've even tried.
metadata.summary: A first-generation college student initially felt lost and behind peers in career planning, almost not applying for internships due to a lack of guidance. A workshop revealed that everyone is "guessing confidently," inspiring them to apply to 27 internships, ultimately securing one critical acceptance despite many rejections.
